speaker: interviewer
text: '96. So you were mentioning that you were going through-- trying to avoid going to Germany from Rafalowka.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in touch with some people that had some knowledge of partisans which were stationed in the forests of Pinsk, which was east of Rafalowka. And the forest started-- was not far from this town. And they said that the Jews were welcome to join. The only thing is now to get out of the town of Rafalowka which the town was surrounded by German guards all over the place, all over. First of all, there was a very important railway station in Rafalowka, which the supplies were going to the Eastern Front. And second, they knew there were a lot of people who run away. And between them would be Jews and all kinds of partisans, and they were afraid. And the partisans were already at that time attacking the Germans all over, so there were guards all over the place. We found out from these people, like I say, that the partisans are maybe about 20 kilometers away, and if you can just cross, like, a border, from-- getting out into the woods, you would be safe. We made a few attempts to, like, to spy on the Germans and the guards, where they were staying, at the railway station, at the road which you can cross. And we saw the guards standing everywhere, and it was impossible to find a place where you could just pass. This young lady was with us, and she was begging us, we shouldn't leave her. She was all alone. So she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know she-- she survived and she was-- I think her name was Bela, but I'm not sure. And she was with us. We tried about two or three days. And one day, we decided we'll make the move. The German guard was standing there, right near where we were crossing, and there was a road going out of the town into a small village that led you to the forest. So the-- all the four of us, and the lady who was with us-- sorry, the three of us now. Myself, Itshak, Chaim, and Yakov-- four brothers and the young lady. We came to the crossing and we were talking between us. Somehow, they suggested that if you speak Polish, it will be easier. We were talking between us Polish. And we passed right-- maybe two feet away from the German guard. He didn't ask us. He didn't touch us. And we just-- we just crossed and went in. We were just-- as soon as we just-- he didn't see us. We started to run. And we started to run. And about half a day later, we were in the-- in the woods. It wasn't Pinsk yet, but really deep in the woods. We went into some Ukrainian families, asked for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, it was a funny remark this lady made. I came in with my brother and knocked on the door. And she looked at us and she said to me in Ukrainian, [UKRAINIAN], which means, are you Jews or are you people?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't mean the remark-- here was really-- a Jew was a Jew, and people were people. So it was an innocent remark really, but it was funny to hear. But she gave us bread. And she--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you answer?

speaker: interviewee
text: We said [UKRAINIAN]. We said, we're Jews. Because what else? We were in the forest. What can we say? And she was very nice because she gave us bread. And we continued on our way deeper into the forest where-- they told us where the partisans were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people. Because that area was never occupied, that area in the forest. The Germans were afraid to go in there. And they've never been occupied. They've never gone into some villages. They were afraid to go in there because the partisans were there, and was in big mud and no roads, or anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in Rafalowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rafalowka, we were about two weeks. Two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually finding out where the partisans were from the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, from the Ukrainians. They told us where the partisans. We arrived to a place where the partisans were there. It was called the unit [NON-ENGLISH], and one was-- another unit was [NON-ENGLISH]. Fedorov-- they called him the Jewish father. He had taken all the Jews and even women with children. And he set up a special unit for women and children, and the fighting unit were in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so Fedorov was the name of a man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he-- he was parachuted from Moscow, and he was leading the unit. And the assistant, which was an officer too, was a Ukrainian man, and he was just, like, a wonderful person. The only thing, when it came to the fighting unit, they said to my brother, Chaim, right away that you must organize to get your own arms. They wouldn't give any arms to the Jewish partisans. I don't know whether to the others they gave. And my brother joined a unit, which they went near the railway station. They attacked a German guard-- a couple of guards there-- And they killed them and they got their arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which unit did he join?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fighting unit in the Fedorov--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fedorov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Battalion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Diadia Petia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diadia- Diadia Petia was another unit, which he led. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the name of a person as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Actually, Petia, was his name, and Diadia means an uncle. I don't even remember his last name. And he was a Russian man. And since I was only 13-- at that time, almost 13 years old-- they wouldn't take kids into the unit. They left them with the women. But I didn't have a mother or nobody else, so I said I would join the unit and I'll help you take care of the horse, or fight, or spy. So Diadia Petia took me in, and I was his, really, adjutant, he called it. I took care of the horse. And I, later on, went dressed as a Ukrainian kid into the villages and find out where the Germans are stationed so the partisans could attack them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the partisans Jewish or non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish, most of them. Were a few Jewish. Were all Russians-- some Ukrainian, mostly Russians. And a lot of them were prisoners of war which escaped from the Germans. And they were so angry at the Germans, what they did to them, that they really were fighting bitterly, and from Moscow, they were getting supplies already in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did to prisoners of war? They tortured them. They killed them. They-- you know, they burned their families. They killed their families. And many Ukrainians turned against them, too. But most of them were still with the Ukrainian nationalists, which we'll call [NON-ENGLISH], which were really nationalists fighting for their own independence. And I used to be very good also climbing. So there was a burned down church, and I would stand on top of the church, climb up the steps-- which was just the steps left. And I'd climb up on top of the church with binoculars, check if the Germans are coming. Because many times, they used to come and attack the partisans, to try to wipe out the partisans and attack them. Attack them from-- attack us from the air. Many times, we had to move somewhere else. Would come and bomb the forest. And we lived in such-- in the ground-- such bunkers in the ground, we lived, the whole winter of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what-- describe the bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bunker was built like a V unit like this here, dug inside deep, and then covered with all kind of branches-- big branches-- and covered with earth-- leaves and earth. And then we had a stove inside. Built a very primitive stove, and it was very nice and warm. We made such banks and we slept on the banks. And I remember singing with them, and I had a good voice. I used to sing for-- with them and life was very good for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there? How many partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in our unit, the Diadia Petia was 2,000. Fedorov had about 4,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 4,000 people were hiding in the forests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, you're talking about forests. Big, big. Not hiding, really fighting. [LAUGHS] We didn't have to hide because it was just like a military unit. Used to attack the German-- the Germans at the railroad crossings and all kind of special assignments the partisans were getting from Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they able to arm 4,000 people and then have enough backup ammunition to keep on supplying the weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later on we were getting, actually, parachute-- parachuters coming in from Moscow, flying in and dropping ammunition and some armaments. And also, what they took away from the Germans. Attacking the Germans, they took away a lot of arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, yourself, ever kill any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say that I saw, but I did use a gun for a while. But not knowingly, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for almost, yeah, a year, until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were you in contact with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were close by with the units. We always saw each other from time to time, every few weeks or so. We would-- they would come to see me. And I would come to see them. And Diadia Petia, the commander, he was just like a father to me, really. He was really good. I forgot to mention in the story-- my story-- when we came back to Stepan after the war, and my brothers were in the in the police, going after the Nazis that the German-- the Ukrainian Nazis that were killing our families-- killed. I forgot to mention that we did get a hold of the man that took my mother and me and took her to the police station. We did find this guy, and this guy was tried and sentenced. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, when my brother Chaim was killed, Yakov, my brother, was in the police, and he was very upset at that time. So he went into the village where he was killed and there were bandits called [? Malinsk ?] in that village. And he was-- really, got drunk. And he took a machine gun and attacked the whole village, unfortunately, and killed a lot of people there. And Soviet authorities put him to jail. He was in jail and Diadia Petia became the Chief Justice in Rovno, in the city of Rovno, at that time, which was-- Rovno was the main city of the [NONENGLISH]. [SIGHS] And I was in such good terms with Diadia Petia that I was able to go to him. I was-- from school, I went to see Diadia Petia and asked him if he could release my brother. He says, it's very difficult what he did. But I said, look. The rage he was in. They killed my brother. They killed our parents and everybody else, those Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, I'll see what I do. And about a few weeks later, my brother was released. Yakov was released. Unfortunately, later they killed him. But he was released. So going back to Diadia Petia, he was very good to us while we were in the partisans. And later after the war, too, like I said. And in 1944, the Russian-- the Germans were escaping, and the Russians were coming closer and closer to us, which was the happiest days of our life. And then in March, they came and they liberated-- liberated us. The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. We came into the town back to Rafalowka. The Germans were still bombing Rafalowka, but luckily we didn't get hit. And then, we all returned to the town of Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we move on, I wanted to ask you. Throughout this year in the forest, where did you get your food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The partisans used to put on a tax levy on all the farmers in the villages. And they used to come and says, you have to give so much for us. And if somebody didn't want to give, they took it by force. But mainly, they would come into the city hall and speak to the elders of the city. And they say they should mark who has more of something and they would have to give us an amount, like tax-- taxes. They were taxed. Every village were taxed and were taking away food. If not, we just had to go in and take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would somebody not want to give? Were they not friends of the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were not friends of the partisans, especially the Russians. Some of them-- many of them were nationalists. But most of them would rather have the Soviets than the Germans, so they were helping. And they had no choice. They came in and just-- came in at night and just take so many cows, so many ox, or any other food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Walsh, when you were a little boy back in Male Webcze, before-- like in the '30s-- did you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war? Yeah, we were religious. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say yes, I was religious. Yeah. I was-- believed in God, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all these experiences-- losing your mother, being a partisan-- have any effect on your belief?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When-- I remember when were taking us to the ghettos, and-- for the extermination, and my father that-- was a religious man. And he went [INAUDIBLE]. Just the night before the extermination started, the rabbi made a speech that people should not run away from-- from this, that this is something that is happening to us. We don't know why, but so we should stay together and go through Kiddush HaShem. My father didn't really believe in it. I don't think so. He didn't believe in it, and he didn't-- he was a religious man, but I don't know what was in his heart. But myself, I turned completely against religion. I remember later on after the war, there were synagogues and people were going to pray. But I didn't go near it. As a matter of fact, I don't think I wanted to be Jewish anymore for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe in God while you were in the partisans? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't think of such a thing as God because just resentment. I wouldn't say to God, but just to any other religion. It didn't do anything. Like I said before, I didn't feel any different from any other children that I went to school with and everything else. Why did they take us and start killing? For what reason?

speaker: interviewer
text: All this time when you were in the partisans, did you use your own name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The partisans? No. I forgot to mention that in the-- in the village, they called me [INAUDIBLE], which was Ukrainian for-- whatever, [INAUDIBLE], Mike, or whatever. It doesn't matter. And when I was in [INAUDIBLE], we all had to have Polish names. My lady called me [INAUDIBLE], and the neighbors didn't know I was Jewish and I had to go with her to church. It was very nice, and I learned the prayers, and I was fine this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about in the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the partisans? No. We all used our own names. And there were some antisemitic outbursts and so on, but in general, we were well treated in the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with some of these outbursts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some of them-- not in our units, but in some other units, they killed some Jewish guys and they threw them out from the partisans and so on, because there were some antisemites.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a kid, did you have a nickname? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What would your parents or brothers and sister call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Just [INAUDIBLE]. Just like a Yiddish name for Mordechai.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Mordechai, OK. So after March, 1944, you said you went back to Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, 1944, we-- we went back to Stepan. Don't ask me why, because really there was nothing to go to. Everything was taken away from us. Everybody was killed. But we didn't know of any other place. And besides, somehow in our blood, in our minds was to take revenge on the people that-- Ukrainian people that took us to the killing. And some of them, like my-- the guy that took me and my mother, and so on. And we wanted to bring them all to justice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I know it was a mistake because a lot of people went to bigger cities and they got out of this mess. Maybe my brothers Yakov and Chaim would have lived if we did leave. But we didn't have any other place to go to, so that's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan, we were myself, my brother, Yitshak, was there. And like I said, Chaim and Yakov, which were, as I told you before, were killed. In 19-- Chaim in 1944, October, 1944, and Yakov in 1947, after I already left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to that girl that came with you originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard of her until later on. We heard from somebody that she was in the-- she survived and she was in the-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you-- so how long were you in Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Stepan until my brother, Chaim, was killed. And then, since he was the main supporter and I went to school, I continued my schooling, only went to-- in 1944, I continued into grade five. When he was killed, I decided that I should go, maybe, and learn a trade right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the City Communist party, or whatever they're called-- that city there-- gave me permission to go to learn for a projectionist on movies. And I went to Lwow. They sent me there to Lwow, and I finished there for a projectionist of movies, about six months' course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, I returned-- I had to return back and to work for the government. And they gave me a helper, which we had a motor, a generator, because there was no electricity in the villages. And we had to go into the villages and show Soviet films in all the villages. And I did this for about six months, and then I figured there was no future in it. And at the same time, the Ukrainian bandits were attacking from all over-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every Russian or Ukrainian or Jewish people that were involved with the Soviets. So I decided to go back to Lwow, and I went back and enrolled into a trade school. And in the trade school, I met a friend of mine, Shlomo Grinshtain, that was also from Stepan. And we went back together there. We were learning. And we met some Jewish people and they told us there was a Jewish organization in Lwow that would maybe help us. We went to the Jewish organization, and I met there, standing outside, a Jewish man who was in the Communist party, and he invited us into his house. When we were in his house, he said, this is a secret. I'm in the Communist party, but you kids have no future here. A lot of Jewish people that go to Poland. And there, they are sent to America or other countries that they can go to school and so on. We decided to go to Poland. In order to go to Poland, we had to go to a Polish ambassador in Lwow. We went over to see the Polish ambassador. They asked us for some documents. I went and I got some-- my birth certificate. When we came there, we were all nervous and upset because they were drilling us and questioning us. We didn't speak such a good Polish. And they only let people go that were Polish born or Polish citizens. So they didn't believe we were really born in Poland. And finally, there was another man that came in and-- I was all upset. I started to cry. And they said, let the kids go to Poland. Let them go. So they gave us a visa. And then-- I think it was in the spring of 1947-- we left from Poland to-- from Lwow to Poland, and we were taken off in Cracow. In Krakow, we were met by Jewish organizations, all kinds of Zionist and religious-- it was a religious group that approached me. They asked if my parents were religious. I said yes, so they took me right into a religious orphanage. I stayed there for a couple days and I didn't like it there. And I met a friend that was in a orphanage from HaNoar HaTzioni. And I went there and I stayed in Cracow for about four or five months. I couldn't sit still, so I went to work in a library. Actually, not in a library. It's like a bookstore, and I was delivering books. People were buying books and I was delivering the books. And then, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Cracow, they moved us to Bytom, a city in upper Silesia, Poland, where we were in a-- called, like, a kibbutz, or a kinderheim, orphanage. And in there, I went and registered to go to school in [INAUDIBLE] school, which was a trade school and a regular schooling-- high school courses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took my courses. I did very well in the school. The people were very nice to us. The principal was a Communist Jew, but he was very good. He was dedicated. And when we-- when I finished, I got my certificate as a machinist and auto mechanic. After I finished this course, a friend of mine was going to school in a city of Gliwice, also in Poland. And that was a special school-- pre-university school where younger men that didn't have a chance during the war to finish high school in a normal way, they would prepare you in one year to go to university. I went over and registered. And fortunately, at that time, the principal was a Jewish man by the name of Mr. Friedman. His wife was a math teacher, and they were without any children and they took to me very much, and they were very nice to me. And I lived in Gliwice and went to school there, for preparation for university, and I wanted to study engineering. At that same time, HaNoar HaTzioni still was active, and I was going to meetings in the Zionist organization. I learned quite a bit about Zionism, and was eager to go to fight for Israel, because it was already-- in 1948, beginning of '48, the war broke out in Israel for independence. And I was eager to go. But for some reason, they wouldn't give me a passport to go, and I continued school. My friend decided to leave Poland because they were going to, I don't know, recruit him to the army or something. His parents and-- his father was a doctor, so he was able to get his papers and went to Israel. And I got my papers later on. And I went to Paris because a friend of mine that went to school with me-- George Guc, G-U-C, and his parents-- had some family in Paris and in New York. And they were taken over to Paris, so they wanted me to stop in Paris and maybe go with them to United States. I arrived to Paris, and-- and I stayed with another group, which were a Zionist group. Some of them were going to Israel. Some, I don't know where else. And we stayed in a hotel. And this friend of mine, with his parents, were trying to convince me to go with them. And his uncle had made them papers to go to New York. At the time, the war was very-- going very bad in Jerusalem. I remember reading and listening to the radio for Israel. And a speaker came from-- I think [INAUDIBLE] and some others-- a big hall in Paris, and I went to listen to him. And I got all excited, and I registered to go to Israel, to fight for Israel. About three days later, I was shipped-- went to Marseilles. And from Marseilles, we were on the boat [INAUDIBLE], which was already an Israeli boat. And with [INAUDIBLE], we arrived to Haifa. That was already the end of '48. I think the station was [INAUDIBLE]. In [INAUDIBLE], I-- we stayed about maybe three days. And that was the time after the Latrun fighting, where Israel lost a lot of soldiers near Jerusalem-- Latrun. The Seventh Brigade of the Palmach, and [INAUDIBLE] came to recruit some more young men to fill in the unit. And he picked me and some other guys to go to the Palmach, into unit. And the next day, we were shipped to Beersheba. And we had training for a couple days, and went on the Jordanian front near Beersheba, [INAUDIBLE]. The front line was [INAUDIBLE]. That was Passover of 1949. I remember Passover because we didn't get any matzohs. The Jordanians cut off the road that led our supplies. And then, our soldiers came and they opened up. And one of my friends was killed in [INAUDIBLE] while we were on a mission. I felt very good. I felt excited about it, because finally, I am a free man and I fight for my own country. And I had nobody else, as far as I know. I didn't know what happened to my brothers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Ukraine.
